text.title: RAG Failure Taxonomy Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that define the closed 7-category taxonomy and how reviewers apply it consistently across triage and weekly review.

rule.statement: Every RAG failure maps to exactly one of seven categories: retrieval-miss (correct passage not retrieved at all), retrieval-misranked (correct passage retrieved but below the top-k cutoff), chunk-boundary (correct doc retrieved but the answer spans a chunk boundary or sits in the wrong chunk), generation-grounding-fail (retrieved context contains the answer but the generator hallucinated or contradicted it), stale-doc (correct passage retrieved but the underlying document is out of date), prompt-instruction-leak (system prompt or template forced the wrong framing), downstream-tool-fail (RAG returned correct text but the tool-use / function-calling step failed).

rationale.source: empirical RAG failure analysis at multiple production teams; RAGAS / Pinecone failure taxonomies

rule.rationale: A closed taxonomy is the only mechanism that produces consistent diagnoses across reviewers. Free-text labels generate hundreds of near-duplicate tags and defeat aggregation. Seven categories is the empirical sweet spot — granular enough to map to distinct fix paths, coarse enough to be memorised.

example.title: Category classification
example.code: failure_id: f-2026-05-19-114 question: "What is the refund window on annual plans?" correct_answer: "30 days from charge date for annual subscriptions." actual_answer: "We do not currently offer refunds." classification: generation-grounding-fail evidence: retrieval: passage P-447 contains the refund policy, retrieved at rank 2 context_to_model: passage was included in the prompt generation: model output contradicts the passage, no quote from P-447 fix_path: tune generator-prompt; add refusal-on-contradiction rule

rule.statement: Each failure is assigned exactly ONE category — the root cause that, if fixed, would resolve the failure. When evidence supports multiple categories, the reviewer picks the upstream cause (retrieval before generation, chunking before retrieval, KB before chunking). Secondary contributing factors are recorded in a `contributing_factors` field but do not change the primary category.

rationale.source: root-cause analysis discipline; multi-tag noise in failure logs

rule.rationale: Multi-category tagging defeats aggregation: a single failure tagged as both retrieval-miss and generation-grounding-fail double-counts in the weekly report. Picking the upstream cause aligns the fix-path with the actual remediation; contributing-factor field preserves the nuance without polluting the primary classification.

rule.statement: Each entry includes evidence sufficient to confirm the category: the retrieval log (chunk IDs + ranks + similarity scores), the generation prompt (context window), the actual model output, and the source-of-truth answer. Failures without this evidence are tagged `evidence-incomplete` and skipped from the weekly aggregation until backfilled.

rationale.source: evidentiary discipline in defect tracking; common RAG-triage failure patterns

rule.rationale: Without evidence, classifications drift to whatever the reviewer feels most often. Required evidence forces the reviewer to look at the retrieval log and the prompt, which catches mis-classifications.

rule.statement: Each category maps to a default fix path: retrieval-miss → retriever (embedding model, hybrid search, query rewriting); retrieval-misranked → reranker; chunk-boundary → chunker (size, overlap, hierarchical); generation-grounding-fail → generator prompt (refusal rules, citation requirement); stale-doc → knowledge-base refresh cadence; prompt-instruction-leak → system prompt audit; downstream-tool-fail → tool-use prompt + schema. Recommendations may override the default with justification.

rationale.source: targeted-remediation research; observed RAG fix patterns

rule.rationale: The fix-path mapping turns a category from a label into an action. Weekly review surfaces the top category; the fix path tells the team which subsystem to invest in. Without this mapping, the taxonomy becomes a museum.

rule.statement: On Fridays the team runs the weekly aggregation: category frequency, top-3 categories by count, recommended fix path for each. The report is posted to the team channel. Fixes commit in the following week aligned with the top category; weekly progress measured by category-frequency change.
rule.rationale: Failure data without a weekly review feeds nothing. The Friday rhythm makes the data actionable. Categories that consistently dominate week-over-week signal where the system needs structural change, not incremental patches.

list.item: retrieval-miss — the right passage was NEVER in the top-k retrieved set
retrieval-misranked — right passage was retrieved but below the top-k cutoff or with low rank
chunk-boundary — chunking split the answer across boundaries, or the right chunk lacks crucial context
generation-grounding-fail — context contained the answer, model hallucinated or contradicted it
stale-doc — source document is outdated, system retrieved it correctly, answer is stale
prompt-instruction-leak — system prompt forced wrong framing (over-refusal, persona drift, format violation)
downstream-tool-fail — answer was correct but the tool-use / function-calling step failed
